prompt:
Every handler in our admin plane starts with forty lines of copy-pasted parameter checking, each one slightly different, each one inventing its own error JSON. I want that gone. Build me `reqvalid.py`: a WSGI middleware, stdlib only (this runs in the appliance, there is no pip), with one entry point `wrap(app, rules)` that returns a new WSGI callable.

`rules` is a plain dict keyed by `(METHOD, path)` tuples -- exact string match on PATH_INFO, no patterns, that's deliberate. The value is a list of field specs, each a dict: `name`, `in` (one of `query` / `header` / `body`), `type` (one of `int` / `str` / `bool`), plus optional `required` (default false), `default`, `min` / `max` (ints only), `choices` (allowed values after coercion).

Behavior per request:
- If `(method, path)` isn't in the table, the middleware is invisible: call straight through, do NOT read the request body, do NOT set any environ key. Same method on a declared path with a different method entry counts as undeclared.
- Query params come from QUERY_STRING. A declared param appearing more than once is an error (code `duplicate`). Any query param that isn't declared is an error (code `unexpected`) -- strict on purpose, typos should fail loudly. Values are strings, so coerce: `int` via integer parse, `bool` accepts true/1/false/0 in any case, anything else is code `type`. `str` passes through.
- Headers are looked up case-insensitively (they arrive however the client felt like spelling them). Same string coercion rules. Headers are NEVER strict -- clients send whatever they want; only declared ones are checked. Missing required header: code `required`.
- Body fields: only touch the body at all if the route declares at least one body field. Empty or absent body counts as `{}`. A body that doesn't parse as JSON, or parses to a non-object, is the single problem `{"name": "body", "in": "body", "code": "json"}`. Otherwise fields must already be the right JSON type -- no string-to-int coercion in a JSON body, and a JSON boolean is NOT an int even though Python thinks bool subclasses int. Wrong type: code `type`. Undeclared body keys: code `unexpected`.
- After type checks: `min`/`max` violations are code `range`, `choices` violations are code `choice`. Missing optional with a `default` gets the default injected; missing optional without one is simply absent.
- Collect EVERY problem in the request, don't stop at the first. If anything failed, respond `400 Bad Request` with content type `application/problem+json` and an RFC 7807 problem document: `{"type": "urn:problem-type:request-validation", "title": "Request validation failed", "status": 400, "invalid_params": [...]}` -- those four keys and values exactly (extra keys like `detail` are your call). Each invalid_params entry carries at least `name`, `in`, `code`; the list is sorted by name then by in. The inner app must not run.
- If everything passed, put the coerced params in `environ["reqvalid.params"]` as a dict keyed by spec name and call the inner app. Whatever the inner app returns -- status, headers, body -- goes back untouched.

The acceptance contract is `test_reqvalid.py`, which boots wrapped apps over loopback HTTP and drives them with http.client. `python3 test_reqvalid.py` must pass; the test file is off limits.
